lines: +# Hashes that share the same set of field names can store those names once in a
+# shared template instead of repeating them in every key, saving memory when
+# many hashes use the same field set. A hash with at least this many fields is
+# automatically converted to the template encoding on write. Hashes that use
+# field expiration (e.g. HEXPIRE) are never converted, since templates target
+# stable field sets. The default 0 disables automatic conversion.
+# Conversion is one-way: once a hash uses the template encoding it keeps it for
+# the life of the key, even if fields are later removed (e.g. via HDEL) and the
+# count drops back below the threshold. Changing this directive at runtime is
+# applied lazily: it only affects a key on its next write (e.g. HSET); existing
+# Template encoding pays off when many keys share a field set that stays mostly
+# stable. Adding or removing a field name moves the key to a different template,
+# so a workload that frequently changes field names pays a template lookup or
+# creation on every such write instead of saving memory.
+# Upper bound for automatic template conversion. A hash with more fields than
+# this is not converted to the template encoding, even if it meets
+# hash-min-template-entries. This is useful to keep very wide hashes out of the
+# shared template registry. The default 0 disables the upper bound. Like the
+# minimum, this is applied lazily on the next write and never converts existing
+# The two directives above convert hashes on the write path (HSET and friends).
+# The two below convert hashes while an RDB is being loaded. They only do
+# something when the RDB has no templates in it; if the RDB already contains
+# templates, it is loaded as is and these are ignored. So this is a one-time
+# conversion that lets an old RDB (saved before templates existed) gain the
+# memory savings on load. This is mainly because of the
+# which allows Redis to disassemble inefficient templates. Limiting the
+# conversion to the initial upgrade avoids repeatedly assembling and
+# While such an RDB loads, a plain hash is converted to a template when its
+# field count is at least the minimum (and at most the maximum, if the maximum
+# is non-zero). Hashes that use field expiration (e.g. HEXPIRE) are never
+# converted. The default 0 disables this (and the maximum bound); a hash that
+# would have qualified is left plain and may instead be converted later, on its
+# A template shared by only a few keys costs more memory than it saves. After
+# the load-time conversion above, this is the minimum number of keys a template
+# must have to be kept: at the end of the load, any template used by fewer keys
+# than this is broken back into plain hashes.
+# Safety valve: while loading, Redis tracks how many templates have fewer keys
+# than this threshold. Once at least 1000 templates have been created, if more
+# than half of them are still below the threshold, Redis assumes that an RDB
+# with many distinct field sets, each used by only a few keys, is a poor fit for
+# templates and stops creating new templates partway through the load. From that
+# point, only hashes whose field set matches a template already created during
+# this load are template-encoded; the rest stay plain. If that happens, keys
+# will be partially converted to templates.
+# Like the two above, this applies only when loading an RDB that has no
+# templates. The default 0 disables it, keeping every template the load created.
# Lists are also encoded in a special way to save a lot of space.
# The number of entries allowed per internal list node can be specified
# as a fixed maximum size or a maximum number of elements.

+{MAKE_CMD("discard","Removes a single session-local fieldset by name.","O(N) where N is the number of session-local fieldsets.","8.10.0",CMD_DOC_NONE,NULL,NULL,"hash",COMMAND_GROUP_HASH,HIMPORT_DISCARD_History,0,HIMPORT_DISCARD_Tips,1,himportDiscardCommand,3,0,ACL_CATEGORY_HASH,HIMPORT_DISCARD_Keyspecs,0,NULL,1),.args=HIMPORT_DISCARD_Args},

+{MAKE_CMD("discardall","Removes all session-local fieldsets for the connection.","O(N) where N is the number of session-local fieldsets.","8.10.0",CMD_DOC_NONE,NULL,NULL,"hash",COMMAND_GROUP_HASH,HIMPORT_DISCARDALL_History,0,HIMPORT_DISCARDALL_Tips,1,himportDiscardallCommand,2,0,ACL_CATEGORY_HASH,HIMPORT_DISCARDALL_Keyspecs,0,NULL,0)},
